title: Ansible Module Failure Rate by Module Type
app: Custom jsonlines callback to HEC, or `community.general.splunk` pattern
spl:
index=iac sourcetype="ansible:callback" OR sourcetype="ansible:result"
| eval mod=coalesce(task_action, module_name, action)
| rex field=mod "^(?P<mod_base>[a-zA-Z0-9._-]+)"
| eval failed_task=if(status="failed" OR result="failed" OR failed>0,1,0)
| stats count as tasks, sum(failed_task) as fails by mod_base, playbook
| eval fail_rate=round(100*fails/nullif(tasks,0),2)
| where fail_rate > 3 AND tasks >= 20
| sort -fail_rate
description: Package managers, copy, and service modules fail for different root causes; aggregating failure rate by module family highlights brittle integrations (proxy repos, SELinux, Windows WinRM) before they become outage-sized playbook failures.
value: Prioritizes engineering fixes on the modules that drive the most wasted operator time and rerun churn.
implementation: Normalize `task_action` to strip arguments (`apt: name=foo` → `apt`). Exclude `debug` and `set_fact` from denominators if they inflate counts. Join to CMDB for OS family when correlating `yum` vs `apt` failures.
visualization: Bar chart (fail_rate by module), heatmap (module × playbook), pie of failure share.
grandmaExplanation: We group failures by the type of tool step that broke—like installing packages versus copying files—so you see which kinds of steps keep failing instead of only seeing that a big script failed.
